parentQuestion: A patient arrives in the ER after a diving accident with spinal cord trauma at C6. They can shrug their shoulders and flex their elbows, but cannot extend their elbows or move their wrists or fingers. Sensation is present in the lateral arm and forearm but absent in the hand. Which anatomical principle explains this pattern?

scaffolds.q: The spinal cord is organized into segments - cervical, thoracic, lumbar, and sacral.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! 8 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral segments - each sending out spinal nerves to specific body regions.

scaffolds.q: Each spinal segment controls specific muscles (called a myotome).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! C5 controls shoulder muscles, C6 controls wrist extensors, C7 controls elbow extensors, and so on. Predictable mapping.

scaffolds.q: Each spinal segment receives sensation from specific skin areas (called a dermatome).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Dermatomes are strips of skin innervated by specific spinal levels. C5-C6 covers the lateral arm, C7-C8 covers the hand.

scaffolds.q: Spinal cord injury at a specific level affects everything BELOW that level.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The lesion cuts off segments below from the brain. Above the lesion = preserved. At and below = affected.

scaffolds.q: C5 controls shoulder shrug and elbow flexion (biceps).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! That's why this patient can still shrug and flex elbows - C5 is above the C6 lesion.

scaffolds.q: C7 controls elbow extension (triceps) - below C6.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The patient can't extend elbows because C7 is disconnected from the brain by the C6 lesion.

scaffolds.q: C8-T1 control the hand muscles - also below C6.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! No wrist or finger movement because C8-T1 segments are below the injury level and can't receive motor commands.

scaffolds.q: The pattern of preserved vs. lost function tells you the LEVEL of spinal cord injury.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This is segmental innervation and dermatome mapping in action - the anatomical principle that explains the pattern.

scaffolds.q: The answer is SEGMENTAL INNERVATION AND DERMATOME MAPPING - each level controls specific muscles and skin regions.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not spinal shock (that's temporary reflex loss). Not hemisection (that causes different deficits on different sides). Not decussation (that's about left/right crossing). Segmental organization explains the level-specific pattern.
